text: The prevalence of low energy availability (LEA) in female and male track and field athletes ranges between 18% and 58%, with the highest prevalence among endurance and jump event athletes.
Low energy availability in male athletes may result in reduced testosterone levels, reduced libido, and impaired training capacity.
Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea as a consequence of LEA has been reported in 60% of elite middle- and long-distance female athletes and 23% of elite female sprinters.
population: elite female track and field athletes

text: Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea is associated with impaired bone health, elevated risk for bone stress injury, and cardiovascular disease.
Low energy availability negatively affects recovery, muscle mass, neuromuscular function, and increases the risk of injuries and illness.
Athletes with long-term LEA may be weight stable yet have impaired physiological function due to metabolic and physiological adaptations that prevent further weight loss.

Treatment for athletes with LEA requires a multidisciplinary team approach including a sport physician, sports dietitian, physiologist, and psychologist, with the primary goal of increasing energy availability.
